name: Knex.js
what_it_does: Promise-based SQL query builder for Node.js with a chainable API, schema migrations, and connection pooling — sits between raw SQL and a full ORM.
use_cases: Build type-safe parameterized SQL queries programmatically without string concatenation in agent data pipelines
Manage schema migrations with knex.schema.createTable() and a versioned migration directory for deployment automation
Construct dynamic WHERE clauses with chained .where()/.orWhere()/.whereIn() calls based on runtime agent inputs
Execute raw SQL via knex.raw() with binding placeholders when the query builder abstraction is insufficient
Handle connection pooling and multi-dialect support (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, MSSQL) from a single interface
not_for: Projects that need entity-level abstractions with relations and lifecycle hooks — use an ORM like TypeORM or Sequelize instead
Developers who prefer writing raw SQL directly — the query builder adds little value over a simple pg or mysql2 driver in that case
GraphQL or REST API generation — knex only handles the query layer and provides no schema-to-API tooling
best_when: You need SQL-level control and portability across dialects without the overhead of a full ORM, especially for complex dynamic query construction.
avoid_when: Your team needs entity-relation modeling with associations, eager loading, and model lifecycle hooks that a query builder does not provide.

auth.notes: Library — credentials are provided in the knex({ client, connection }) config object or via connection string.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Errors include the generated SQL and binding values in development mode, which aids debugging but can leak data — agents must configure debug:false in production.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (upsert) is dialect-specific; knex.raw() is needed for full upsert semantics on PostgreSQL — no built-in idempotent insert abstraction.
known_agent_gotchas: knex() returns a query builder, not a Promise — agents must call .then() or await to actually execute the query, not just build it
knex.schema migrations are ordered by filename timestamp; generating migration files out of order will apply them incorrectly
Connection pool exhaustion is silent — queries will hang indefinitely rather than throwing if pool.max is reached and acquireTimeoutMillis is not set
knex.raw() does NOT escape identifiers (table/column names) — agent-generated identifiers must be whitelisted, not passed directly
Transaction rollback on error requires explicit try/catch with trx.rollback(); unhandled exceptions inside a transaction do not auto-rollback

security_score_components.security_notes: Query builder uses parameterized bindings by default, preventing SQL injection; knex.raw() with ?? identifier escaping is limited — agents must validate identifiers externally.
